name: Mickey Roker
abstract: Granville William "Mickey" Roker (September 3, 1932 – May 22, 2017) was an American jazz drummer.

quotes.text: I don’t think there is such a thing. Because if you are a dedicated player, you’re going to play your heart out. I don’t care if you’re playing in the parking lot. You know what I’m saying? Sometimes you play a Jazzmobile where you’re riding in the street and you’re playing for people in different neighborhoods. That don’t mean that you should give up less there then you would give up in Carnegie Hall. Your dedication is sad if you do that. So that’s, you know, there’s no easy audience and there’s no hard audience. You know what I’m saying? What makes the audience is the player.
quotes.caption: General philosophy on dedication to playing
quotes.doc_title: Mickey Roker interviewed by Michael Woods, Majesty of the Seas, May 31, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Percy Heath
about_quotes.evidence: Mickey played with us almost a year… Mickey replaced Connie. He made most of the record date there with MJQ and Friends… we did a whole year almost with(out him?)
about_quotes.doc_title: Percy Heath — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Junior Mance Part I
about_quotes.evidence: I had my trio working with Joe, which was Bob Cranshaw and Mickey Roker and myself.
about_quotes.doc_title: Junior Mance Part I interviewed by Monk Rowe and Michael Woods, New York City, New York, July 27, 1995 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Lew Tabackin
about_quotes.evidence: I played with Duke Pearson’s band which was wonderful, because we had a great rhythm section, with Mickey Roker and Bob Cranshaw.
about_quotes.doc_title: Lew Tabackin interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 17, 1999

about_quotes.speaker: Akiyoshi Toshiko
about_quotes.evidence: And uh, I had Lew, myself, Mickey Roker, and Lew’s very good friend called, um, Bob... That’s how I was invited, for quartet.
about_quotes.doc_title: Akiyoshi Toshiko — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Louie Bellson
about_quotes.evidence: Mickey and I were the two drummers. We wound up playing fours.
about_quotes.doc_title: Louie Bellson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Ray Drummond
about_quotes.evidence: I’m thinking Wynton Kelly and Paul Chambers, Jimmy Cobb or Philly Joe and Wynton Kelly and Paul Chambers and Percy Heath playing with Mickey Roker, all this king of thing...
about_quotes.doc_title: Ray Drummond interviewed by Monk Rowe, Toronto, Canada, January 9, 2003
